content:
This appears to not be supported. In other words, it's impossible to have a mutex (which needs to be pinned) wrap something that also needs to be pinned.

This makes the following pattern impossible to implement:

Mutex<Opaque<bindings::foo>> where bindings::foo contains e.g.: a list_head or similar.

Having a mutex wrap a bindgen type might be handy if we want to enforce that all access to the bindgen field be serialized, because it makes it impossible to get a *mut Foo otherwise.

A similar (but much worse) solution would be:

#[pin_data]
struct Foo {
  #[pin]
  inner: Opaque<bindgen::foo>,
  #[pin]
  protects_inner: Mutex<()>
}

That is clearly inferior, as we now must manually make sure that protects_inner is locked before accessing inner, instead of relying on the type system.

cc @Benno Lossin @Alice Ryhl
we at some point talked about making the T in Mutex<T> structurally pinned, but didn't follow through with it
most likely because there was no user

Benno Lossin said:

we at some point talked about making the T in Mutex<T> structurally pinned, but didn't follow through with it

most likely because there was no user

Yeah, this is not the first time I face this situation, which is what pushed me to finally start this thread. So if there wasn't a user, there is one now :)

If the result of this discussion turns out to be that we'd like to get this supported, let me know how I can help.
let's hear from the rest of the @team and see if anyone has any objections
we'd probably have to change Lock & make all locks pin their inner value, or split the locking API back into the different parts
wait, structurally pinned means if Mutex<T> is pinned, so is T right? But I don't think we need T to _always_ be pinned? We'd just like to support having it be pinned if needed

Benno Lossin said:

we'd probably have to change Lock & make all locks pin their inner value, or split the locking API back into the different parts

or introduce a new PinMutex<T>, but that is most likely confusing...

Daniel Almeida said:

wait, structurally pinned means if Mutex<T> is pinned, so is T right? But I don't think we need T to _always_ be pinned? We'd just like to support having it be pinned if needed

how would you differentiate between two instances of Mutex<T>, one of which pins T and the other doesn't?
I guess it is fine to pin T. If T: Unpin everything should work as before, right?

Andreas Hindborg said:

I guess it is fine to pin T. If T: Unpin everything should work as before, right?

yes

If T: Unpin everything should work as before, right?

Ah right, I missed this part.
Structurally pinning data makes sense. In fact my v. early prototype of pin-init does that https://github.com/nbdd0121/pin-init/blob/trunk/examples/pthread_mutex.rs#L86
should we then just do it for Lock?
I think we should
you will then make .lock() return a Pin<Guard> instead of a Guard, because Guard impl DerefMut
That doesn't sound super ergnomic!
how so? and what would you suggest instead?
It's just generally I don't like to write out Pin very often. We could implement DerefMut conditionally if T: Unpin and provide a as_mut() that provides Pin<&mut T>?
I guess that's identical to Pin<Guard>..

where would you have to write Pin?

let mut guard = lock.lock();
Good point, I guess type inference is good enough
and if you have a function that takes a Guard<'_, T> now, that function should just take a &mut T or impl DerefMut<Target = T> instead
Taking impl feels like an overkill
I think we should have more types that declare themselves to be pinned, just like Arc
note that Pin<Guard> is still DerefMut if T is Unpin.
so I don’t think the change affect ergonomics when T is Unpin
asahi has Add Lock::pin_init()
that is unsound
it allows moving the value, because it's not pinning the guard

@Boqun Feng @Benno Lossin I created an issue at https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1181

Let's keep the discussion focused here please.

Can anyone assign that to me in Github btw? I can't.
Benno beat me to it :)

in the issue you say:

Note that Bar is pinned, so having it in a Mutex will not compile at the moment. This is specially undesirable, since Foo is already pinned, and thus, it could trivially enforce that its bar field is pinned as well.

What do you mean by "will not compile at the moment"? The example you gave above does compile and it has the bar field marked as pinned.

You won't be able to instantiate a Foo that pins the Bar in bar, maybe that's what you meant?

Furthermore, there is no support for pinned projections, so the following won't work:

regarding that, I have some good news for you: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/pin-init/pull/75
I will try to put that in v6.18

You won't be able to instantiate a Foo that pins the Bar in bar, maybe that's what you meant?

Yes, this means that in practice the example will not work, correct?
so the wording was indeed bad on my end, but hopefully the meaning is still valid
yeah I just got confused and thought you meant it would trip up earlier (ie when just defining the types, which shouldn't happen)
Edited to make it clearer

Benno Lossin said:

Furthermore, there is no support for pinned projections, so the following won't work:

regarding that, I have some good news for you: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/pin-init/pull/75

You can put a link to that PR at the issue that Daniel just created.
